article.title: Trends in the prevalence of metabolically healthy and unhealthy obesity in the US adult population: analysis of eight NHANES cross-sectional survey cycles, 1999-2014
article.authors: Wen Y, Liu T, Li S, Gong R, Li C

study_type.notes: Cross-sectional analysis of 8 NHANES survey cycles (1999-2014). Participants: 16,459 adults aged 20+ years. Analysis of metabolically healthy obesity (MHO) and metabolically unhealthy obesity (MUO) trends. NHANES is cross-sectional by design, but repeated cycles 1999-2014 create a trend analysis resembling ecological/cohort approach. Design ambiguity noted.

2.notes: Background: 'accumulating evidence suggests that obesity is not homogeneous...metabolically healthy obesity (MHO)...at much lower risk for cardiometabolic morbidity'

3.notes: Objective: 'to determine the prevalence and trends of different obesity phenotypes among a nationally representative sample'

4.notes: Study design: 'Eight cross-sectional survey cycles...NHANES is a programme of studies designed to assess health and nutritional status'

5.notes: Setting: 'National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES), 1999-2014...eight survey cycles conducted between 1999 and 2014'

6a.notes: Eligibility and selection: '19,537 participants aged 20 years and older from eight survey cycles...3,078 excluded for missing data...final sample 16,459'

7.notes: Outcomes clearly defined: MHO (central obesity + metabolically healthy), MUO (obese + abnormality). Exposures: gender, race/ethnicity, age, education. Confounders: medication use

8.notes: Data collection: 'Trained interviewers conducted household interviews...standardized physical examinations...waist circumference by trained technologist...BP by trained physician...HDL-C and TG measured enzymatically...glucose measured enzymatically'

9.notes: Bias discussion: 'Due to serial cross-sectional design, we are not able to track dynamic changes...did not include insulin resistance or inflammation criteria, potentially limiting detection'

10.notes: No sample size calculation or power analysis described

11.notes: Quantitative variables: 'Waist circumference ≥102 cm men, ≥88 cm women; systolic BP <130; fasting glucose <100; TG <150; HDL-C ≥40 men, ≥50 women'

12a.notes: Statistical methods: 'PROC SURVEYREG procedure...to test for trends across survey cycles...stratified, multistage, probability cluster sampling design...SAS software (V.9.4)'

12b.notes: Subgroup analysis: 'stratified by gender, race/ethnicity, age group, education levels...Differences compared between men and women...among age groups and race/ethnic groups'

12c.notes: Missing data: 'Of 19,537 participants, 3,078 were excluded because of missing data on waist circumference, blood pressure, HDL-C, triglycerides or glucose'

12d.notes: Not applicable - cross-sectional design, no follow-up or matching

13a.notes: Participant flow: 19,537 from 8 cycles, 3,078 excluded, 16,459 analyzed. Table 1 shows characteristics by cycle

13b.notes: Reasons for exclusion: missing data on waist circumference, blood pressure, HDL-C, triglycerides or glucose

14a.notes: Baseline characteristics: Table 1 shows mean age, gender, race/ethnicity, education distribution across survey cycles

14b.notes: Missing data completeness: specified 3,078 excluded for missing data; implicit reporting of data availability

15.notes: Outcome data: Figure 1, Table 2 report prevalence of MHO, MUO across cycles. 'MHO prevalence increased from 11.0% to 15.7% in centrally obese population'

16a.notes: Estimates with precision: Prevalences reported with percentages and p-values. 'Central obesity 45.2% (1999-2000) to 56.7% (2013-2014) (p=0.003)'

16b.notes: Category boundaries: age groups (10-year), education levels (9th grade, high school, college), race/ethnicity categories defined

16c.notes: Absolute prevalence provided but not translated to absolute risk reduction or number needed to treat

17.notes: Secondary analysis: stratified results by gender, age, race/ethnicity, education. Gender differences highlighted with trend analysis

18.notes: Key results: 'MHO prevalence in centrally obese population slightly increased from 11.0% to 15.7%...significant increase among women (7.1% to 13.7%)'

19.notes: Limitations: 'Due to serial cross-sectional design, unable to track dynamic changes...did not include insulin resistance or inflammation...secondary analysis of de-identified data'

20.notes: Cautious interpretation: discusses obesity paradox and need for further research. Acknowledges limitations in generalizability of trends

21.notes: Generalizability: 'nationally representative sample of US adults...high generalisability of study findings...large sample size allowed estimation for minority groups'

22.notes: Funding/conflicts not clearly stated in abstract or methods section
